title: There May Be Three Times More Insect Species Than We Realized
gist: A Cornell-led genetic census of parasitoid wasps in Costa Rica's Área de Conservación Guanacaste, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, indicates global insect diversity is far larger than the current estimate of ~5.5 million species (only ~1.2 million formally described) — the study's headline finding puts the true count at roughly three times prior realized numbers.

insights.claim: A new genetic-census study of parasitoid wasps, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences by Cornell University entomologists, concludes there could be a lot more insect species on Earth than previously thought — the study's own headline finding puts the figure at roughly three times the number currently realized.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
entities: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
insights.so_what: A large upward revision to global biodiversity estimates matters for conservation-finance, biodiversity-offset markets, and bioprospecting theses tied to insect-derived compounds and ecosystem services.
insights.quote: there could be a lot more than we previously thought
insights.locator: according to a study published today in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

insights.claim: Current scientific estimates put global insect diversity at around 5.5 million species, but only around 1.2 million — roughly a fifth — have been formally described and are known to science.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of undescribed biodiversity, a baseline figure relevant to any long-run thesis on ecosystem-service valuation or genomic bioprospecting.
insights.quote: Current estimates range around 5.5 million, but only around 1.2 million are known to science.
insights.locator: But we still don't know how many species there are

insights.claim: Insects' highly adaptable segmented body plans, small size, and quick generation times explain why they are the most diverse and abundant animal group on Earth, present on every continent and biome.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A structural, mechanistic explanation for insect dominance that underlies why undercounting is plausible at this scale — relevant context for biodiversity-data and conservation-tech theses.
insights.quote: highly adaptable segmented body plans, small sizes, and quick generation times

insights.claim: Cornell University entomologists reached their diversity estimate by taking a genetic census of parasitoid wasps in Costa Rica's Área de Conservación Guanacaste (ACG), a large nature preserve in the country's northeast, combining collection of newly emerged adult wasps with tent-trap sampling of other insects.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Establishes a specific, replicable genetic-survey methodology in a biodiversity hotspot that could be extended to other reserves — relevant for conservation-genomics and biodiversity-data-tooling providers.
insights.quote: taking a genetic census of parasitoid wasps in Costa Rica’s Área de Conservación Guanacaste (ACG)

insights.claim: The parasitoid wasps sampled in the study lay their eggs inside caterpillars; the larvae then devour the host caterpillar from the inside before emerging as adults, a life cycle researchers exploited to capture and identify new wasp diversity.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Explains the biological mechanism behind the sampling method, supporting confidence in the dataset underlying the diversity estimate.
insights.quote: the larvae devour the caterpillars from the inside and emerge as adults
insights.locator: These wasps lay their eggs in caterpillars

top_entities: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
